text: The solubility product of \(\text{Ni(OH)}_2\) at 298 K is \(2 \times 10^{-15}\) mol\(^3\) dm\(^{-9}\). The pH of its aqueous saturated solution is
explanation:
For the dissolution equilibrium: \(\text{Ni(OH)}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{Ni}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^-\). If solubility \(= S\) mol/L, then \([\text{Ni}^{2+}] = S\) and \([\text{OH}^-] = 2S\).

\[K_{sp} = [\text{Ni}^{2+}][\text{OH}^-]^2 = S \cdot (2S)^2 = 4S^3 = 2 \times 10^{-15}\]

\[S = \left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-15}}{4}\right)^{1/3} \approx 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/L}\]

\[[\text{OH}^-] = 2S = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}\]

\[\text{pOH} = -\log(1.6 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 5\]

\[\text{pH} = 14 - 5 = 9\]
name: Questions Item 1

text: Acidity of \(\text{BF}_3\) can be explained on the basis of which of the following concepts?
options: Bronsted-Lowry as well as Lewis concept
explanation: In \(\text{BF}_3\), boron is \(sp^2\) hybridised and has only 6 electrons in its valence shell — it is electron deficient. It can **accept a lone pair** of electrons from a Lewis base, making it a Lewis acid. Since it neither donates nor accepts a proton, it cannot be classified under the Arrhenius or Bronsted-Lowry concept. Hence, only the **Lewis concept** explains the acidity of \(\text{BF}_3\).
name: Questions Item 2

text: Which of the following will have the highest pH?
explanation:
pH increases with increasing basicity. Comparing the four options:

- Saturated NaOH solution: \(\text{pH} \approx 15\) (strongly basic)
- 1 M HCl: \(\text{pH} \approx 0\) (strongly acidic)
- Human saliva: \(\text{pH} = 6.4\) (slightly acidic)
- Lemon juice: \(\text{pH} \approx 2.2\) (acidic)

Therefore, the **saturated NaOH solution** has the highest pH.
name: Questions Item 3

text: When equal volumes of \(\text{Ca}^{2+}\) and \(\text{F}^-\) solutions are mixed, in which of the following will precipitation NOT occur? \((K_{sp}\text{ of }\text{CaF}_2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-10})\)
explanation:
Precipitation occurs only when the ionic product \((K_{ip})\) exceeds \(K_{sp}\). For option (a):

\[K_{ip} = [\text{Ca}^{2+}][\text{F}^-]^2 = (10^{-2}) \times (10^{-5})^2 = 10^{-12}\]

Since \(10^{-12} < 1.6 \times 10^{-10} = K_{sp}\), the ionic product is less than the solubility product, so **precipitation does not occur** in this mixture.
name: Questions Item 4

text: The solubility of \(\text{A}_2\text{X}_3\) is \(y\) M. Its solubility product is ______ \(\text{M}^5\).
explanation:
For the dissolution: \(\text{A}_2\text{X}_3 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{A}^{3+} + 3\text{X}^{2-}\)

If solubility \(= y\) M, then \([\text{A}^{3+}] = 2y\) and \([\text{X}^{2-}] = 3y\).

\[K_{sp} = [\text{A}^{3+}]^2[\text{X}^{2-}]^3 = (2y)^2 \cdot (3y)^3 = 4y^2 \times 27y^3 = 108y^5\]
name: Questions Item 5

text: The strongest conjugate base among the following is
explanation:
A **weaker acid** produces a **stronger conjugate base**. The acid strength order of the halogen acids is:

\[\text{HI} > \text{HBr} > \text{HCl} > \text{HF}\]

Since \(\text{HF}\) is the weakest acid among these, it produces the strongest conjugate base. Therefore, \(\text{F}^-\) is the strongest conjugate base.
name: Questions Item 6

text: For a reversible reaction, if the concentration of the reactants is reduced to half, the equilibrium constant will be ________.
explanation: The equilibrium constant \(K_{eq}\) is a function of **temperature only**. It is completely independent of the initial concentrations of reactants or products. Changing concentrations will shift the equilibrium position (Q vs K), but the **value of \(K_{eq}\) itself does not change**. Therefore, even if reactant concentration is halved, \(K_{eq}\) remains the same.
name: Questions Item 7

text: The \(pK_a\) of benzoic acid and \(pK_b\) of ammonium hydroxide are 4.25 and 4.75 respectively. The pH of 0.1 M solution of ammonium benzoate will be
explanation:
Ammonium benzoate is a salt of a **weak acid** (benzoic acid, \(pK_a = 4.25\)) and a **weak base** (ammonium hydroxide, \(pK_b = 4.75\)). For such a salt, the pH is given by:

\[\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}(pK_a - pK_b)\]

\[= 7 + \frac{1}{2}(4.25 - 4.75) = 7 + \frac{(-0.50)}{2} = 7 - 0.25 = 6.75\]
name: Questions Item 8

text: For the reaction \(\text{NO}_2 + \text{CO} \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{CO}_2\), one mole of \(\text{NO}_2\) and 2 moles of \(\text{CO}\) were kept in a 1 L vessel. If at equilibrium 25% of the initial amount of \(\text{CO}\) is consumed, the equilibrium constant \(K_p\) is
explanation:
Assuming volume = 1 L. Setting up the ICE table:

|  | \(\text{NO}_2\) | \(\text{CO}\) | \(\text{NO}\) | \(\text{CO}_2\) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | 1 | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| Change | −0.5 | −0.5 | +0.5 | +0.5 |
| Equilibrium | 0.5 | 1.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 |

(25% of 2 mol CO = 0.5 mol consumed)

\[K_C = \frac{[\text{NO}][\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{NO}_2][\text{CO}]} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{0.5 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.25}{0.75} = \frac{1}{3}\]

Since \(\Delta n_g = (1+1)-(1+1) = 0\), \(K_p = K_C(RT)^0 = K_C = \dfrac{1}{3}\)
name: Questions Item 9

text: The pH of \(10^{-8}\) M HCl solution is
explanation: HCl is a strong acid, so its solution is always **acidic (pH < 7)**. At \(10^{-8}\) M, the concentration is extremely low, so the \(\text{H}^+\) contribution from water (\(\approx 10^{-7}\) M) cannot be ignored. The total \([\text{H}^+] \approx 1.005 \times 10^{-7}\) M, giving \(\text{pH} \approx 6.98\). This value lies **between 6 and 7**, confirming the solution remains acidic.
name: Questions Item 10

text: Which acid among the following has the highest \(pK_a\) value?
explanation:
\(pK_a = -\log K_a\). A **higher \(pK_a\)** means a **weaker acid**. The \(pK_a\) values are:

| Acid | \(pK_a\) |
|---|---|
| HF | 3.1 |
| HCl | −6.0 |
| HBr | −9.0 |
| HI | −9.5 |

Since \(\text{HF}\) has the highest \(pK_a\) value (3.1), it is the **weakest acid** in this group.
name: Questions Item 11

text: The ionic product of water ________ with increase in temperature.
explanation:
The auto-ionisation of water is an **endothermic** process:

\[\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^-\]

By Le Chatelier's Principle, increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium to the right, producing more \(\text{H}^+\) and \(\text{OH}^-\) ions. Therefore:

\[K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-]\]

**increases** with increase in temperature.
name: Questions Item 12

text: The solubility of AgBr with solubility product \(5.0 \times 10^{-13}\) at 298 K in 0.1 M NaBr solution would be
explanation:
In 0.1 M NaBr, \([\text{Br}^-] = 0.1\) M due to the **common ion effect**.

For \(\text{AgBr} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}^+ + \text{Br}^-\):

\[K_{sp} = [\text{Ag}^+][\text{Br}^-] = 5.0 \times 10^{-13}\]

\[[\text{Ag}^+] = \frac{K_{sp}}{[\text{Br}^-]} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-13}}{0.1} = 5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ M}\]

The solubility of AgBr in 0.1 M NaBr is \(5 \times 10^{-12}\) M — much lower than in pure water because of the common ion effect.
name: Questions Item 13

text: For the reaction \(2\text{NH}_3(s) + \text{CO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)\), find the value of equilibrium constant at 295 K. Given: standard Gibbs energy change \(\Delta G^\circ = -13.9\) kJ mol\(^{-1}\).
explanation:
Using the Gibbs free energy relation:

\[\Delta G^\circ = -2.303\,RT\log K_C\]

\[-13.9 \times 10^3 = -2.303 \times 8.314 \times 295 \times \log K_C\]

\[\log K_C = \frac{13900}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 295} = \frac{13900}{5641.5} \approx 2.46\]

\[K_C = 10^{2.46} \approx 288.4 \approx 2.88 \times 10^2\]
name: Questions Item 14

text: Calculate the molar ratio \(\dfrac{[\text{Acid}]}{[\text{Salt}]}\) of a weak acid HA \((K_a = 10^{-6})\) and its salt with a strong base, so that the pH of the buffer solution is 6.
explanation:
Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation:

\[\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]}\]

Here, \(pK_a = -\log(10^{-6}) = 6\) and \(\text{pH} = 6\):

\[6 = 6 + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]}\]

\[\log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} = 0 \implies \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} = 1\]

\[\therefore \frac{[\text{Acid}]}{[\text{Salt}]} = 10^{-1} = 0.1\]
name: Questions Item 15

text: For a reversible reaction, if the concentration of reactants is increased, the equilibrium constant of the reaction ________.
explanation: The equilibrium constant is a function of **temperature only**. It is independent of changes in concentration of reactants or products. When reactant concentration is increased, the reaction shifts in the forward direction to re-establish equilibrium, but the **numerical value of \(K_{eq}\) does not change**. Hence the equilibrium constant remains constant.
name: Questions Item 16

text: The supply of oxygen to tissues by blood can be explained by
explanation:
The transport of oxygen by haemoglobin involves the equilibrium:

\[\text{Hb} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{HbO}_2\]

At high \(\text{O}_2\) partial pressure (lungs), equilibrium shifts right (oxygen is loaded). At low \(\text{O}_2\) pressure (body tissues), it shifts left (oxygen is released). This shift of equilibrium in response to changing conditions is explained by **Le Chatelier's Principle**.
name: Questions Item 17

text: Calculate the equilibrium constant of the reaction \(\text{Cu}(s) + 2\text{Ag}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Ag}(s)\), given \(E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0.46\) V.
explanation:
Using the relationship between \(E^\circ_{\text{cell}}\) and equilibrium constant:

\[\log K = \frac{nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}}{2.303\,RT}\]

Here \(n = 2\) (2 electrons transferred):

\[\log K = \frac{2 \times 96500 \times 0.46}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298} = \frac{88780}{5706} \approx 15.56\]

\[K = 10^{15.56} \approx 3.92 \times 10^{15}\]
name: Questions Item 18

text: \(K_{sp}\) for \(\text{CaSO}_4\) is \(9 \times 10^{-6}\). The minimum volume of water needed to dissolve 1 g of \(\text{CaSO}_4\) at 298 K is
explanation:
For \(\text{CaSO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}\):

\[K_{sp} = S^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \implies S = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}\]

Molar mass of \(\text{CaSO}_4 = 40 + 32 + 64 = 136\) g/mol

Solubility in g/L \(= 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 136 = 0.408\) g/L

Volume to dissolve 1 g \(= \dfrac{1}{0.408} \approx 2.45\) L
name: Questions Item 19

text: For the species \(\text{HSO}_4^-\) and \(\text{NH}_3\), their conjugate acids respectively are
explanation:
A **conjugate acid** is formed when a base accepts a proton (\(\text{H}^+\)):

- \(\text{HSO}_4^-\) (base) + \(\text{H}^+\) → \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\) (conjugate acid)
- \(\text{NH}_3\) (base) + \(\text{H}^+\) → \(\text{NH}_4^+\) (conjugate acid)

Therefore, the conjugate acids are \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\) and \(\text{NH}_4^+\).
name: Questions Item 20

text:
The ionisation constants of some weak acids are given below. Their increasing order of acid strength is:

Formic acid (HCOOH): \(K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}\)
Hypochlorous acid (HClO): \(K_a = 3.0 \times 10^{-8}\)
Nitrous acid (\(\text{HNO}_2\)): \(K_a = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}\)
Hydrocyanic acid (HCN): \(K_a = 4.9 \times 10^{-10}\)
explanation:
A **larger \(K_a\)** means a **stronger acid**. Ranking by \(K_a\) in increasing order:

\[\text{HCN}\;(4.9 \times 10^{-10}) < \text{HClO}\;(3.0 \times 10^{-8}) < \text{HCOOH}\;(1.8 \times 10^{-4}) < \text{HNO}_2\;(4.5 \times 10^{-4})\]

So the increasing order of acid strength is: **HCN < HClO < HCOOH < \(\text{HNO}_2\)**.
name: Questions Item 21

text: Calculate the mass of AgCl precipitated when 25 mL of 35% solution of \(\text{AgNO}_3\) and 25 mL of 11.6% solution of NaCl are mixed.
explanation:
In 25 mL of 35% \(\text{AgNO}_3\): mass \(= \frac{35}{100} \times 25 = 8.75\) g

Moles of \(\text{AgNO}_3 = \frac{8.75}{170} = 0.0515\) mol

In 25 mL of 11.6% NaCl: mass \(= \frac{11.6}{100} \times 25 = 2.9\) g

Moles of \(\text{NaCl} = \frac{2.9}{58.5} = 0.0496\) mol

\(\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} \downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3\)

NaCl is the limiting reagent (0.0496 mol).

Mass of \(\text{AgCl} = 0.049 \times 143.5 \approx 7\) g
name: Questions Item 22

text:
The number of species from the following that can act **both** as Bronsted acids and Bronsted bases is:
\(\text{HCl},\; \text{ClO}_4^-,\; \text{OH}^-,\; \text{H}^+,\; \text{H}_2\text{O},\; \text{HSO}_4^-,\; \text{SO}_4^{2-},\; \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4,\; \text{Cl}^-\)
explanation:
A species that can act as both Bronsted acid AND base is **amphoteric**. Checking each:

- \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\): can donate \(\text{H}^+\) (→ \(\text{OH}^-\)) **or** accept \(\text{H}^+\) (→ \(\text{H}_3\text{O}^+\)) ✓
- \(\text{HSO}_4^-\): can donate \(\text{H}^+\) (→ \(\text{SO}_4^{2-}\)) **or** accept \(\text{H}^+\) (→ \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\)) ✓
- All others (HCl, \(\text{ClO}_4^-\), \(\text{OH}^-\), \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\), \(\text{Cl}^-\), \(\text{SO}_4^{2-}\)) can only act as either acid or base.

Hence, **2 species** — \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\) and \(\text{HSO}_4^-\) — are amphoteric.
name: Questions Item 23

text: In which of the following will the solubility of AgCl be minimum?
explanation:
AgCl dissolves as:

\[\text{AgCl} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^-\]

In 0.1 M KCl, the \(\text{Cl}^-\) ion from KCl is a **common ion**. This increases \([\text{Cl}^-]\) and by Le Chatelier's Principle, the equilibrium shifts left, suppressing the dissolution of AgCl. This **common ion effect** reduces solubility to a minimum. \(\text{KNO}_3\) has no common ion and actually increases solubility slightly (salt effect).
name: Questions Item 24

text: Given: \(\text{NH}_3 + \text{Ag}^+ \rightleftharpoons [\text{Ag(NH}_3)]^+\), \(K_1 = 1.6 \times 10^3\) and \([\text{Ag(NH}_3)]^+ + \text{NH}_3 \rightleftharpoons [\text{Ag(NH}_3)_2]^+\), \(K_2 = 6.8 \times 10^3\). The equilibrium constant for \(\text{Ag}^+ + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightleftharpoons [\text{Ag(NH}_3)_2]^+\) is
explanation:
The overall reaction is obtained by **adding** the two given equilibria. When equilibrium reactions are added, their constants are **multiplied**:

\[K_3 = K_1 \times K_2\]

\[= (1.6 \times 10^3) \times (6.8 \times 10^3)\]

\[= 10.88 \times 10^6 = 1.088 \times 10^7\]
name: Questions Item 25

text: A buffer solution is prepared by mixing 10 mL of 1.0 M acetic acid and 20 mL of 0.5 M sodium acetate, then diluted to 100 mL. The pH of the buffer is \((pK_a\) of acetic acid \(= 4.76)\)
explanation:
Millimoles of acetic acid \(= 10 \times 1.0 = 10\) mmol

Millimoles of sodium acetate \(= 20 \times 0.5 = 10\) mmol

Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation:

\[\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} = 4.76 + \log\frac{10}{10} = 4.76 + \log 1 = 4.76 + 0 = 4.76\]
name: Questions Item 26

text: The equilibrium constant at 850 K for \(\text{N}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}(g)\) is 0.5625. The equilibrium concentration of NO is \(3.0 \times 10^{-3}\) M. If the equilibrium concentrations of \(\text{N}_2\) and \(\text{O}_2\) are equal, the concentration of \(\text{N}_2\) in M is
name: Questions Item 27

text: The solubility product of a sparingly soluble salt \(\text{A}_2\text{B}\) is \(3.2 \times 10^{-11}\). Its solubility in mol L\(^{-1}\) is
explanation:
For \(\text{A}_2\text{B} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{A}^+ + \text{B}^{2-}\), let solubility \(= S\).

\[[\text{A}^+] = 2S, \quad [\text{B}^{2-}] = S\]

\[K_{sp} = (2S)^2 \cdot S = 4S^3 = 3.2 \times 10^{-11}\]

\[S^3 = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-11}}{4} = 8 \times 10^{-12}\]

\[S = \sqrt[3]{8 \times 10^{-12}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}\]
name: Questions Item 28

text: The value of \(K_C\) for \(\text{CO}_2(g) + \text{C}(s) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{CO}(g)\) at \(T\) K is 0.036. If the equilibrium concentration of \(\text{CO}_2(g)\) is 0.004 M, the concentration of \(\text{CO}(g)\) in mol L\(^{-1}\) is
explanation:
The activity of solid C is 1, so:

\[K_C = \frac{[\text{CO}]^2}{[\text{CO}_2]}\]

\[0.036 = \frac{[\text{CO}]^2}{0.004}\]

\[[\text{CO}]^2 = 0.036 \times 0.004 = 1.44 \times 10^{-4}\]

\[[\text{CO}] = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/L}\]
name: Questions Item 29

text: 50 mL of 0.02 M NaOH is mixed with 50 mL of 0.06 M acetic acid. The pH of the resulting solution is \((pK_a = 4.76,\; \log 5 = 0.70)\)
explanation:
Millimoles of NaOH \(= 50 \times 0.02 = 1\) mmol

Millimoles of \(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = 50 \times 0.06 = 3\) mmol

\(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\)

After reaction: Salt = 1 mmol, Acid remaining = 2 mmol → **acidic buffer**

\[\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} = 4.76 + \log\frac{1}{2}\]

\[= 4.76 + \log(5 \times 10^{-1}) = 4.76 + 0.70 - 1 = 4.46\]
name: Questions Item 30

text: At 1000 K, the partial pressures of \(\text{CO}_2(g)\) and \(\text{CO}(g)\) for \(\text{CO}_2(g) + \text{C}(s) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{CO}(g)\) at equilibrium are 0.15 and 0.60 bar respectively. The \(K_C\) is approximately
name: Questions Item 31

text: At T(K), the ionisation constant of ammonia in solution is \(2.5 \times 10^{-5}\). The pH of 0.01 M ammonia solution and the ionisation constant of its conjugate acid respectively are \((\log 2 = 0.30)\)
explanation:
**Step 1 — Conjugate acid constant:**

\[K_a(\text{NH}_4^+) = \frac{K_w}{K_b} = \frac{10^{-14}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4 \times 10^{-10}\]

**Step 2 — pH of 0.01 M NH₃:**

\[\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{K_b}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01}} = 0.05\]

\[[\text{OH}^-] = C\alpha = 0.01 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}\]

\[\text{pOH} = -\log(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 4 - \log 5 = 4 - 0.70 = 3.3\]

\[\text{pH} = 14 - 3.3 = 10.7\]
name: Questions Item 32

text: The equilibrium partial pressures of \(\text{CO}(g)\) and \(\text{CO}_2(g)\) for \(\text{CO}_2(g) + \text{C}(s) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{CO}(g)\) at 1000 K are 0.66 and 0.15 bar respectively. The equilibrium constant \(K_C\) is approximately
name: Questions Item 33

text: 20 mL of 0.2 M NaOH solution is added to 40 mL of 0.2 M acetic acid solution. The pH of the resulting solution is \((pK_a\text{ of }\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = 4.8)\)
explanation:
Millimoles of NaOH \(= 20 \times 0.2 = 4\) mmol

Millimoles of \(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = 40 \times 0.2 = 8\) mmol

\(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\)

Salt formed = 4 mmol; remaining acid = 4 mmol → **equal moles** → buffer.

\[\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} = 4.8 + \log\frac{4}{4} = 4.8 + 0 = 4.8\]
name: Questions Item 34

text: 18.4 g of \(\text{N}_2\text{O}_4\) was placed in a 1 L vessel at 400 K and allowed to attain equilibrium: \(\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)\). If the total pressure at equilibrium is 10.64 bar, the approximate \(K_p\) is \((R = 0.083\text{ L bar K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1})\)
explanation:
Initial pressure of \(\text{N}_2\text{O}_4\):

\[p = nRT = \frac{18.4}{92} \times 0.083 \times 400 = 0.2 \times 33.2 = 6.64 \text{ bar}\]

Let \(p_i\) = pressure of \(\text{N}_2\text{O}_4\) that dissociates. Total pressure:

\[p_T = (p - p_i) + 2p_i = p + p_i = 10.64\]

\[p_i = 10.64 - 6.64 = 4.00 \text{ bar}\]

At equilibrium: \(p_{\text{N}_2\text{O}_4} = 6.64 - 4 = 2.64\) bar; \(p_{\text{NO}_2} = 2 \times 4 = 8\) bar.

\[K_p = \frac{(p_{\text{NO}_2})^2}{p_{\text{N}_2\text{O}_4}} = \frac{64}{2.64} \approx 24.24\]
name: Questions Item 35

text: If the pH of a buffer solution containing 0.1 M of a monoacidic base and 0.01 M of its salt is 10.5, the \(pK_a\) of the conjugate acid is
explanation:
Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation:

\[\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]}\]

\[10.5 = pK_a + \log\frac{0.01}{0.1} = pK_a + \log(10^{-1}) = pK_a - 1\]

\[pK_a = 10.5 + 1 = 11.5\]
name: Questions Item 36

text: The stepwise stability constants for a complex are: \(K_1 = 1.0 \times 10^4\), \(K_2 = 1.0 \times 10^3\), \(K_3 = 1.0 \times 10^3\), \(K_4 = 1.0 \times 10^2\). The overall stability constant \(\beta_4\) for \(M + 4L \rightleftharpoons ML_4\) is
explanation:
The overall stability constant is the **product of all stepwise stability constants**:

\[\beta_4 = K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3 \times K_4\]

\[= (1.0 \times 10^4)(1.0 \times 10^3)(1.0 \times 10^3)(1.0 \times 10^2)\]

\[= 1.0 \times 10^{4+3+3+2} = 1.0 \times 10^{12}\]
name: Questions Item 37

text: At 1000 K, the equilibrium constant \(K_C\) for \(2\text{NOCl}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)\) is \(4.0 \times 10^{-6}\) mol L\(^{-1}\). The \(K_p\) (in bar) at the same temperature is \((R = 0.083\text{ L bar K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1})\)
explanation:
\(\Delta n_g = \text{moles of gaseous products} - \text{moles of gaseous reactants} = 3 - 2 = 1\)

\[K_p = K_C(RT)^{\Delta n} = K_C \times RT\]

\[= (4.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.083 \times 1000)\]

\[= 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 83 = 3.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ bar}\]
name: Questions Item 38

text: If the \(pK_a\) of acetic acid and \(pK_b\) of dimethylamine are 4.76 and 3.26 respectively, the pH of dimethyl ammonium acetate solution is
explanation:
Dimethyl ammonium acetate is a salt of a **weak acid** (acetic acid, \(pK_a = 4.76\)) and a **weak base** (dimethylamine, \(pK_b = 3.26\)).

\[\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}(pK_a - pK_b)\]

\[= 7 + \frac{1}{2}(4.76 - 3.26) = 7 + \frac{1.50}{2} = 7 + 0.75 = 7.75\]
name: Questions Item 39

text: The pH of a buffer formed by mixing 30 mL of 0.1 M \(\text{NH}_4\text{OH}\) and 30 mL of 1 M \(\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}\) is 8.6. The \(pK_b\) of \(\text{NH}_4\text{OH}\) is
explanation:
\[\text{pOH} = 14 - \text{pH} = 14 - 8.6 = 5.4\]

For a basic buffer:

\[\text{pOH} = pK_b + \log\frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]}\]

\[5.4 = pK_b + \log\frac{1}{0.1} = pK_b + \log 10 = pK_b + 1\]

\[pK_b = 5.4 - 1 = 4.4\]
name: Questions Item 40

text: The solubility products of AB, \(\text{A}_2\text{B}\) and \(\text{AB}_3\) are \(4.0 \times 10^{-20}\), \(3.2 \times 10^{-11}\) and \(2.7 \times 10^{-31}\) respectively. The increasing order of their solubility is
name: Questions Item 41

text: Arrange the aqueous solutions of \(\text{CuSO}_4\) (I), NaCN (II), KCl (III) in increasing order of pH.
explanation:
- **\(\text{CuSO}_4\)**: salt of strong acid (\(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\)) and weak base (\(\text{Cu(OH)}_2\)) → **acidic**, pH < 7
- **KCl**: salt of strong acid (HCl) and strong base (KOH) → **neutral**, pH = 7
- **NaCN**: salt of strong base (NaOH) and weak acid (HCN) → **basic**, pH > 7

Increasing pH order: \(\text{CuSO}_4 < \text{KCl} < \text{NaCN}\), i.e., **I < III < II**
name: Questions Item 42

text: For reaction I: \(\frac{1}{2}\text{N}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{NO}_2(g)\), \(K = X\) and reaction II: \(2\text{NO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g)\), \(K = Y\). The equilibrium constant \(Z\) for reaction III: \(\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g)\) is
explanation:
From reaction I: \(X = \dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{[\text{N}_2]^{1/2}[\text{O}_2]}\), so \([\text{N}_2]^{1/2}[\text{O}_2] = \dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{X}\)

From reaction II: \(Y = \dfrac{[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]}{[\text{NO}_2]^2}\), so \([\text{N}_2\text{O}_4] = Y[\text{NO}_2]^2\)

For reaction III:

\[Z = \frac{[\text{N}_2][\text{O}_2]^2}{[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]} = \frac{\left([\text{N}_2]^{1/2}[\text{O}_2]\right)^2}{[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{X}\right)^2}{Y[\text{NO}_2]^2} = \frac{[\text{NO}_2]^2}{X^2 \cdot Y[\text{NO}_2]^2} = \frac{1}{X^2 Y}\]
name: Questions Item 43

text: At 1000 K, the equilibrium constant \(K_p\) for \(2\text{NOCl}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)\) is \(4.157 \times 10^{-4}\) bar. The \(K_C\) (in mol L\(^{-1}\)) is \((R = 0.083\text{ L bar K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1})\)
name: Questions Item 44

text: If the ionisation constant of hypochlorous acid (HOCl) is \(2.5 \times 10^{-5}\), the pH of its 1.0 M solution is \((\log 5 = 0.7)\)
explanation:
For a weak acid with \(\alpha \ll 1\):

\[K_a = C\alpha^2 \implies \alpha = \sqrt{\frac{K_a}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\]

\[\text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{OCl}^-\]

\[[\text{H}^+] = C\alpha = 1 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}\]

\[\text{pH} = -\log(5 \times 10^{-3}) = -\log 5 + 3 = -0.7 + 3 = 2.3\]
name: Questions Item 45

text: At T(K), the equilibrium constant for \(\text{H}_2(g) + \text{I}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{HI}(g)\) is 49. If \([\text{H}_2]\) and \([\text{I}_2]\) at equilibrium are \(2.0 \times 10^{-2}\) M and \(8.0 \times 10^{-2}\) M respectively, \([\text{HI}]\) at equilibrium in mol L\(^{-1}\) is
name: Questions Item 46

text: If the pH of 0.10 M monobasic acid at 298 K is 5.0, the value of \(pK_a\) at the same temperature is
explanation:
\[\text{pH} = 5.0 \implies [\text{H}^+] = 10^{-5} \text{ M} = x\]

For dissociation \(\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-\):

\[K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} = \frac{x^2}{C - x} \approx \frac{(10^{-5})^2}{0.10}\]

\[(x \ll C, \text{ so } C - x \approx C)\]

\[K_a = \frac{10^{-10}}{0.10} = 10^{-9}\]

\[pK_a = -\log(10^{-9}) = 9\]
name: Questions Item 47

text: From the list: (i) \(\text{CaCO}_3\), (ii) \(\text{MgSO}_4\), (iii) \(\text{BaCl}_2\), (iv) \(\text{Sr(NO}_3)_2\), (v) \(\text{MgBr}_2\), (vi) \(\text{MgCl}_2\), the oxoacid salts of group II elements are
options: i, ii, iii, iv, v, vi
explanation:
Oxoacid salts are derived from **oxoacids** (acids that contain oxygen). Checking each:

- \(\text{CaCO}_3\): salt of \(\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3\) (oxoacid) ✓
- \(\text{MgSO}_4\): salt of \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\) (oxoacid) ✓
- \(\text{Sr(NO}_3)_2\): salt of \(\text{HNO}_3\) (oxoacid) ✓
- \(\text{BaCl}_2\), \(\text{MgBr}_2\), \(\text{MgCl}_2\): salts of HCl and HBr which are **not oxoacids** ✗

Therefore the oxoacid salts are **(i), (ii), (iv)**.
name: Questions Item 48

text: At 1000 K, the equilibrium partial pressures of \(\text{CO}(g)\) and \(\text{CO}_2(g)\) for the reaction \(\text{CO}_2(g) + \text{C}(s) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{CO}(g)\) are 0.66 bar and 0.15 bar respectively. The equilibrium constant \(K_C\) approximately is
name: Questions Item 49

text: At T(K), if the ionisation constant of ammonia is \(2.5 \times 10^{-5}\), then for a 0.01 M ammonia solution the \([\text{OH}^-]\) at equilibrium is
explanation:
For \(\text{NH}_4\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-\):

\[\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{K_b}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01}} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.05\]

\[[\text{OH}^-] = C \times \alpha = 0.01 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}\]

This gives \(\text{pOH} = -\log(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.3\) and \(\text{pH} = 14 - 3.3 = 10.7\).
name: Questions Item 50
